titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell, 5 December 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
Summary of the present letter:
Letter from Willie to his mother.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
5/12/17 My dear Mamy No news. I was delighted
to get your splendid parcel this
morning containing such a
beautiful pheasant. It came in
perfect condition and we eat it for
lunch and i intend to finish it
off tonight. Also the fruit
chocolate & in fact everything in the
parcel was most acceptable. The weather is very cold but
fine and I have lots of warm
clothes. If you have another
couple of pairs of socks, I could
do with them now as the great
difficulty here is washing, in fact
if I remain in these parts I will try
sending small parcles of washing
home. The difficulty is to get things
dried. I can get them washed in
any shell hole but I can never
get them really dry. I am at present 40 feet below
ground level in a boche dug-out.
It is much better than being in
a cold trench. I was delighted to get Daddys
long letter yesterday too. We
have been on the move all the time
lately. That parcel could not possibly have
come at a better time - & again
thanks vey much indeed for it.
I have not run out of writing paper
but accidentally left it packed up
in my kit so have only my
note book to write in till I get
back to my kit. Best love to all
Your most affect Son
Willie P.S. Bobby seems to be getting on
A.1. now. His writing is
wonderfully improved.
